metadata.summary: Test-Driven Development with LLM assistance: Red-Green-Refactor cycle, agent hooks, and when TDD pays off vs costs more.

content.title: TDD Workflow
content.summary: Covers the Red-Green-Refactor loop adapted for LLM-assisted development, why tests-as-spec reduce total tokens, PostToolUse hooks that enforce the RED step, per-behavior TDD cycle scripting, and the conditions under which TDD costs more than it saves (exploratory spikes, UI prototypes).

why.p: LLMs naturally skip the RED step — they write implementation and tests together, producing tests that prove the implementation rather than specify behavior. A structured TDD hook and per-behavior loop forces correct ordering: failing test first, minimal implementation second, refactor third. This yields higher-confidence suites with less total rework.

list.item: Starting a new feature or module where behavior is well-defined
Writing business logic where correctness is critical
Enforcing RED step discipline when an agent tends to skip it
Setting up CLAUDE.md PostToolUse hooks for TDD enforcement
Explaining TDD workflow to an agent working on Python/JS/Go code
Exploratory spikes or prototypes where spec is unknown
UI/layout work where visual feedback drives design
Performance optimization (benchmark-driven, not test-driven)
Rapid throwaway scripts

section.name: red-green-refactor-with-llm-assistance
section.p: The three TDD phases adapted for LLM-assisted development, why tests-as-spec save tokens, and common agent failure modes.

section.name: the-three-phases

list.item: RED — Write a failing test that specifies one behavior. Run it. Confirm it fails for the right reason.
GREEN — Write the minimal implementation to make the test pass. No extras, no premature generalization.
REFACTOR — Improve the implementation without changing behavior. Tests must still pass.

section.rule: Never write implementation before a failing test exists. This is the most violated rule in LLM-assisted TDD.
One behavior per RED-GREEN-REFACTOR cycle. Do not batch multiple behaviors into one cycle.

section.name: why-tests-as-spec-save-tokens
section.statement: Tests are the most precise specification format for LLMs.

rationale.p: A failing test communicates: the function signature, expected inputs, expected output, and boundary conditions — all in one artifact. An LLM generating implementation from a test needs less clarification than from a prose requirement.

list.item: Prose requirement → test → implementation: 3 steps, 2 ambiguity points
Test → implementation: 1 step, 0 ambiguity points
Typical token savings: 20-40% fewer back-and-forth clarification turns

antipattern.description: Agent skips RED step — writes implementation and test together
antipattern.reason: Tests written after implementation confirm the implementation, not the specification. The test may pass but miss edge cases. Enforce RED step with a hook or explicit prompt: "Write a failing test first, do not write implementation yet."

antipattern.description: Agent writes overly broad implementation in GREEN step (premature generalization)
antipattern.reason: GREEN should be the minimal code to pass the current test only. Generalization happens in REFACTOR, driven by the next RED test.
REFACTOR must not change behavior. If new behavior is needed, start a new RED-GREEN cycle.

list.item: Exploratory spikes — spec is unknown until you build it; write tests after exploration is done.
UI layout work — visual output drives design; use screenshots and manual review instead.
Performance optimization — benchmark-driven, not test-driven.
Throwaway scripts — not worth the investment.

section.name: tdd-agent-hooks-and-workflow
section.p: PostToolUse hook configuration for TDD enforcement, CLAUDE.md snippet, and the per-behavior TDD loop structure.

section.name: per-behavior-tdd-loop
section.rule: For each behavior to implement, follow this strict sequence:

list.item: 1. Write one failing test for the behavior (RED)
2. Run the tests — confirm failure message matches the expected behavior
3. Write minimal implementation (GREEN)
4. Run the tests — confirm all pass
5. Refactor if needed — run tests again to confirm no regression
6. Commit the test + implementation together
7. Pick next behavior — repeat

section.rule: Never proceed to step 3 without seeing the test fail first. If it passes without implementation, the test is wrong.

section.name: claude-md-tdd-enforcement
section.rule: Add this to your project's CLAUDE.md to remind agents of TDD order:

example.code:
## TDD Workflow
For every new function or method: 1. Write the failing test FIRST. Run it: `pytest tests/test_<module>.py -x` 2. Confirm: test output shows FAILED with the expected error (not an import error). 3. Write implementation. Run tests again: must show PASSED. 4. Refactor if needed. Tests must still pass. 5. Commit both test and implementation together.
NEVER write implementation before a failing test exists.

section.name: posttooluse-hook-for-tdd
section.rule: Configure a PostToolUse hook in settings.json to auto-run tests after every file write.
section.note: The hook runs after every Write/Edit. When writing a test file (test_*.py), it immediately runs pytest on that file and shows the result. If the test passes without implementation, the agent sees it and knows the test is wrong.

section.name: tdd-with-multiple-behaviors
section.rule: Prioritize behaviors from most critical to least critical — implement the happy path first, then edge cases, then error paths.
Keep each cycle short — if a behavior requires more than 30 lines of implementation in GREEN, split it into smaller behaviors.

template.purpose: Shell script: run failing test → implement → run passing test → prompt refactor
